Understanding Single(), SingleOrDefault(), Last(), and LastOrDefault()

5 min read
1 hour ago

--

In Part 1 of this series, we explored some of the most commonly used LINQ methods: Where(), First(), and FirstOrDefault().
These methods are great when you need to filter data or retrieve the first matching record from a collection.
But what happens when you're expecting exactly one result?
Or when you need the last item in a collection instead of the first?
That's where Single(), SingleOrDefault(), Last(), and LastOrDefault() come into play.
At first glance, these methods seem very similar. In fact, many beginners use them interchangeably. The problem is that choosing the wrong method can lead to bugs, unexpected exceptions, and difficult-to-debug issues.
Let's break them down one by one.

Sample Data
We'll continue using the same Employee class from Part 1.
public class Employee
{
public int Id { get; set; }
public string Name { get; set; }
public string Department { get; set; }
public decimal Salary { get; set; }
public int Age { get; set; }
}Sample data:
List<Employee> employees = new()
{
new Employee { Id = 1, Name = "Sandip", Department = "IT", Salary = 60000, Age = 24 },
new Employee { Id = 2, Name = "Priya", Department = "HR", Salary = 45000, Age = 27 },
new Employee { Id = 3, Name = "Rahul", Department = "IT", Salary = 70000, Age = 30 },
new Employee { Id = 4, Name = "Neha", Department = "Finance", Salary = 55000, Age = 26 },
new Employee { Id = 5, Name = "Amit", Department = "IT", Salary = 80000, Age = 32 }
};
1. Single()
The Single() method is used when you expect exactly one matching record.
Not the first.
Not the last.
Exactly one.
Imagine you're searching for an employee using a unique Employee ID.
var employee = employees.Single(e => e.Id == 3);Console.WriteLine(employee.Name);
Output:
RahulSince Employee IDs are unique, only one record can match.
This is the perfect use case for Single().

What Happens if No Record Exists?
var employee = employees.Single(e => e.Id == 100);LINQ throws an exception because no matching record exists.

What Happens if Multiple Records Exist?
Imagine this query:
var employee = employees.Single(e => e.Department == "IT");There are three IT employees.
LINQ throws an exception because more than one record matches.
This is exactly what makes Single() valuable.
It protects you from unexpected duplicate data.

When Should You Use Single()?
Use it when:

• Searching by Primary Key

• Employee ID

• Aadhaar Number

• PAN Number

• Email Address (if unique)

• Username (if unique)
Whenever the data should contain exactly one record, Single() is often the best choice.

2. SingleOrDefault()
SingleOrDefault() is the safer version of Single().
It allows zero matches but still enforces that there cannot be more than one match.
Example:
var employee = employees.SingleOrDefault(e => e.Id == 100);Since no employee exists with ID 100:
nullNo exception is thrown.

But Be Careful
This still throws an exception if multiple records match.
var employee = employees.SingleOrDefault(e => e.Department == "IT");Output:
ExceptionWhy?
Because three employees belong to IT.
SingleOrDefault() allows:
✅ Zero matches
✅ One match
❌ Multiple matches

Single() vs SingleOrDefault()
This is a popular interview question.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Single() vs SingleOrDefault() Image
3. Last()
Now let's switch gears.
Sometimes you need the final record in a collection.
That's where Last() comes in.
Example:
var employee = employees.Last();Console.WriteLine(employee.Name);
Output:
AmitBecause Amit is the last employee in the list.

Using a Condition
You can also use conditions.
var employee = employees.Last(e => e.Department == "IT");Console.WriteLine(employee.Name);
Output:
AmitThere are three IT employees:

• Sandip

• Rahul

• Amit
LINQ returns the last matching employee.

What Happens if Nothing Matches?
employees.Last(e => e.Department == "Marketing");Output:
ExceptionJust like First(), Last() expects a result.

When Should You Use Last()?
Common scenarios:

• Most recent transaction

• Latest order

• Last login record

• Latest audit entry

• Newest message

4. LastOrDefault()
LastOrDefault() works similarly to Last().
The difference is how it handles missing data.
Example:
var employee = employees.LastOrDefault(e => e.Department == "Marketing");Output:
nullInstead of throwing an exception, LINQ safely returns the default value.

Why Developers Prefer LastOrDefault()
Applications deal with unpredictable data.
A department may not exist.
A customer may not be found.
An order may not have been placed.
Using LastOrDefault() helps prevent unnecessary runtime exceptions.

Last() vs LastOrDefault()
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Last vs LastOrDefault()
Common Beginner Mistakes
1. Using Single() on Non-Unique Data
Bad:
employees.Single(e => e.Department == "IT");Departments are not unique.
This causes exceptions.

2. Forgetting Null Checks
var employee = employees.SingleOrDefault(e => e.Id == 100);Console.WriteLine(employee.Name);
This causes a NullReferenceException.
Always check for null.

3. Using Last() Without Understanding Collection Order
Remember:
Last() depends entirely on the order of the collection.
If the collection isn't sorted correctly, the result may not be what you expect.

Real-World Interview Tip
A common interview question is:
"When would you choose Single() over First()?"
Answer:
Use Single() when the data should contain exactly one matching record.
Use First() when multiple records may exist, but you only need the first one.
This demonstrates that you understand both data integrity and LINQ behavior.

Key Takeaways
In this part, we explored four important LINQ methods:
✅ Single()
✅ SingleOrDefault()
✅ Last()
✅ LastOrDefault()
You learned:

• When each method should be used

• How they behave when data is missing

• How they handle multiple matches

• Common mistakes developers make

• Real-world scenarios where these methods shine
In Part 3, we'll dive into sorting and projection using:

• OrderBy()

• OrderByDescending()

• ThenBy()

• Reverse()

• Select()

• SelectMany()
These methods will help you transform and organize data like a professional .NET developer.
